context.title: Phase 1 Santa Fe Historic Preservation Plan: Demographic Data (Appendix D)
context.author: Logan Rockefeller Harris

geographies.urban_area: Santa Fe Urban Area — 29 census tracts following the definition used by the City of Santa Fe Long Range Planning Division (tracts 1.01, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10.01, 10.02, 11.02, 11.03, 11.05, 11.06, 11.07, 12.02, 12.03, 12.04, 12.05, 13.01, 13.02, 13.03, 13.04, 103.14, 103.15, 103.16, 104, 105). Excludes partial tract 102.04.
geographies.historic_districts: Santa Fe Historic Districts — estimated from census tract and block data using GIS shapefiles provided by the City of Santa Fe GIS Division.

methodology: Census variables were available at the tract level but not for smaller geographies. Historic district boundaries do not align with census tracts.

For 1990, 2000, and 2010: identified census tracts overlapping with historic districts, then used block-level data to calculate the percentage of housing and population in each tract within the districts. Applied population percentages to person-level data and housing unit percentages to household/housing-level data.
For 1980: block-level data was unavailable; relied on 1990 estimates of the percent of housing and population within historic districts.
For 2018 ACS: block-level data was unavailable; relied on 2010 estimates.

Census block boundaries change over time. Used the Longitudinal Tract Data Base (LTDB) from Brown University to normalize previous years to 2010 census tract geography. Used NHGIS data with LTDB crosswalk files when variables were not available in LTDB.
limitations: The method assumes that the distribution of variable categories (race, income, tenure, etc.) is uniform within a census tract. In reality, residential segregation and zoning may cause uneven distributions within tracts.
Race and ethnicity definitions changed across census years. Some small changes in racial group populations over time may reflect changing definitions rather than actual demographic shifts.
1980 tenure data was missing for several census tracts; renter- and owner-occupied units do not sum to total occupied units for that year.
Housing structure data (Section 7) is based on samples; totals may differ slightly from Section 5.
Cost burden data uses different percentage cutoffs for 1980/1990 (35% threshold) versus 2000-2018 (30%/50% thresholds). These periods are not directly comparable.
Partial census tract 102.04 was excluded from the Urban Area definition due to small population and analytical complexity.

definitions.Latino: Persons of Hispanic origin, of any race (all years).
definitions.White: Persons of White race only, not of Hispanic origin (all years).
definitions.Black: Persons of Black race only, not of Hispanic origin (all years).
definitions.Native American: Definition varies by year: 1980 includes Hispanic origin; 1990+ excludes Hispanic origin; 2000 includes multiracial; 2010/2018 single race only.
definitions.Asian: Includes Pacific Islander. Definition varies: 1980 includes Hispanic origin; 1990+ excludes; 2000 includes multiracial; 2010/2018 single race only.
definitions.Multiracial/Other: 1980-2000: residual category. 2010/2018: persons of two or more races or 'Other,' not of Hispanic origin.
definitions.Seasonal Vacancy: Vacant units used or intended for seasonal, recreational, or occasional use, including timeshares and interval ownership.
definitions.No Cost Burden: Housing costs less than 30% of income (2000-2018 definition).
definitions.Gross Rent: Contract rent plus monthly cost of utilities and fuels.
definitions.Owner Costs: Mortgage payment, real estate taxes, insurance, utilities, fuels, mobile home costs, and condominium fees.

notes.race_definition_changes: Race/ethnicity category definitions changed across census years. Small changes in racial group populations may reflect definitional shifts. See the full definitions table in Appendix D.
notes.1980_tenure_missing: 1980 tenure data was missing for several census tracts. Renter- and owner-occupied units do not sum to total occupied units. Percentages use the sum of renter + owner as denominator.
notes.cost_burden_threshold_change: 1980/1990 use a 35% threshold to define cost burden. 2000-2018 use 30% (moderate) and 50% (extreme). These periods are not directly comparable.
notes.sample_based_data: Housing structure data is based on samples; totals may differ slightly from housing unit counts.
notes.block_estimation: Historic district estimates derived from census tract/block overlap analysis. Assumes uniform distribution of variables within tracts.

narrativeFindings.title: Population Divergence
narrativeFindings.body: The Santa Fe Urban Area grew 69% from 1980 to 2018 (52,058 to 88,054), while the Historic Districts lost 26% of their population (9,952 to 7,389). This divergence indicates that growth in Santa Fe has bypassed the historic core.

narrativeFindings.title: Ethnic Composition Shift in Historic Districts
narrativeFindings.body: The Historic Districts' Latino population fell 61% (4,718 to 1,840) while the Urban Area's grew 68%. Meanwhile, the White share of Historic District population rose from 51% to 72%. This represents a significant demographic transformation of the historic core.

narrativeFindings.title: Income Crossover
narrativeFindings.body: In 1980, the Historic Districts' median household income ($35,529 in 2018 dollars) was well below the Urban Area's ($47,933). By 2010, the districts had surpassed the city, and by 2018 the gap was substantial: $71,294 vs $58,080. This income crossover, paired with declining population, suggests gentrification.

narrativeFindings.title: Rising Vacancy and Seasonal Use
narrativeFindings.body: Historic District vacancy rose from 6% to 30% of all housing units between 1980 and 2018. Seasonal vacancies — units used for occasional or recreational purposes — grew from 61 to 962 units and now account for 56% of all vacant units. This points to a shift from year-round residency toward part-time and investment ownership.

narrativeFindings.title: Tenure Convergence
narrativeFindings.body: In 1980, the Historic Districts were majority-renter (51% renter vs 49% owner). By 2018, renter share had dropped to 39%, nearly matching the Urban Area's 38%. This shift from renter to owner occupancy aligns with rising incomes and property values in the districts.
